figures.gloss: The Pittsburgh haberdasher (1852–1916) whose faith, shaken by hellfire doctrine, was restored by Adventist preaching (Jonas Wendell, 1869). He absorbed Nelson Barbour's chronology, broke with him over the ransom doctrine, and founded Zion's Watch Tower (1879) and the Bible Student movement, publishing the six-volume Studies in the Scriptures.
figures.facet: A founder who built a publishing corporation rather than a church — the Watch Tower Society's printed page, not a clergy or a sacramental system, is the movement's true institutional skeleton from the start.
figures.provenance: Zion's Watch Tower (1879– ); N. H. Barbour & C. T. Russell, Three Worlds (1877); M. James Penton, Apocalypse Delayed (1985).

figures.gloss: The Watch Tower chronology: Daniel 4's 'seven times' read as 2,520 years from Jerusalem's fall — dated by the movement to 607 BCE against the scholarly 587/586 — ending in October 1914, when Christ was enthroned invisibly in heaven as King. God's theocratic kingdom, a real government, now stands ready to destroy all human rule at Armageddon; hence the Witnesses' strict political neutrality.
figures.facet: A rare case of a failed date that was kept while its event moved: visible expectations attached to 1874, 1914, and 1925 each lapsed, yet 1914 survived as an invisible enthronement — with the World War's outbreak retrofitted as its earthly confirmation.
figures.provenance: C. T. Russell, The Time Is at Hand (1889); Watch Tower publications on the 'Gentile Times'; M. James Penton, Apocalypse Delayed (1985).

figures.gloss: Revelation's 144,000 read as a literal, fixed number: the 'anointed' class resurrected to heaven to rule with Christ as kings and priests over a cleansed earth. Since Rutherford's 1935 teaching, all other faithful Witnesses form the 'great crowd' with an earthly paradise hope; at the annual Memorial only the anointed partake of the bread and wine.
figures.facet: A two-tier salvation with a head-count: heaven is a government post of limited enrollment, not a destination — the sharpest inversion of the generic Christian afterlife on the American religious map.
figures.provenance: Revelation 7:4, 14:1–3; J. F. Rutherford, 'The Great Multitude' (1935); George Chryssides, Jehovah's Witnesses: Continuity and Change (2016).

figures.gloss: House-to-house preaching, grounded in Matthew 24:14, organized under Rutherford into the defining practice of the movement: members are 'publishers' who report their hours, working territory maps from Kingdom Halls with The Watchtower and Awake! in hand.
figures.facet: The tradition's liturgy happens on a stranger's doorstep — evangelism occupies the structural place other churches give the sacraments, and membership itself is counted in preachers, not pew-sitters.
figures.provenance: Matthew 24:14; Watch Tower organizational literature (Organized to Do Jehovah's Will); George Chryssides, Jehovah's Witnesses: Continuity and Change (2016).

figures.gloss: The prohibition on blood transfusion, formalized in The Watchtower of 1 July 1945 from Genesis 9:4, Leviticus 17, and Acts 15:28–29: blood is the life itself and belongs to God, so it may not be taken into the body. Hospital Liaison Committees negotiate bloodless treatment; minor blood fractions were later left to individual conscience.
figures.facet: An ancient sacrificial theology of blood-as-life collides head-on with modern medicine — the refusal makes the member's own body the site of witness, the tradition's costliest and most litigated boundary marker.

figures.gloss: The Watch Tower Society's second president (1869–1942), 'Judge' Rutherford — imprisoned in 1918–19 under the Espionage Act over the film/book The Finished Mystery, released and later cleared — who centralized the movement, adopted the name 'Jehovah's Witnesses' at the 1931 Columbus convention, and instituted the publisher/field-service structure that still defines the organization.
figures.facet: The builder who outlasts the founder in institutional memory: Russell supplied the chronology and the break from Christendom, but it is Rutherford's organizational architecture — territories, publishers, hour quotas, a single theocratic center — that turned a Bible Student following into the Witnesses as they exist today.
figures.provenance: Watch Tower Society organizational records and convention proceedings (1931 Columbus, Ohio); M. James Penton, Apocalypse Delayed (1985).

figures.gloss: The battle of 'the great day of God the Almighty' (Revelation 16:14–16), in which Jehovah destroys the present system of human governments and unrepentant wickedness — imminent, and survivable in the flesh for those aligned with God's Kingdom.
figures.facet: The motive engine behind the doorstep: Armageddon is not a distant afterlife concern but the reason the field ministry exists at all — every knock on every door is framed as a last chance to be found on the right side of an imminent, this-worldly war.
figures.provenance: Revelation 16:14–16; Watch Tower literature on Armageddon as imminent divine war; M. James Penton, Apocalypse Delayed (1985).

figures.gloss: The tradition's only annual observance: the yearly commemoration of Christ's death on Nisan 14, at which emblems of unleavened bread and wine are passed to everyone present but consumed only by those professing to be of the anointed 144,000.
figures.facet: A communion built around near-universal abstention: attendance at the Memorial runs far higher than active membership, making it simultaneously the tradition's largest single gathering and the clearest annual demonstration of how small the anointed class actually is.
figures.provenance: Watch Tower Memorial observance literature; George Chryssides, Jehovah's Witnesses: Continuity and Change (2016).

figures.gloss: The Watch Tower Society's own Bible translation, produced by an anonymous committee (New Testament 1950, complete Bible 1961, revised 1984 and 2013), restoring the name 'Jehovah' throughout both testaments and rendering John 1:1c 'the Word was a god.'
figures.facet: Scripture engineered to match doctrine rather than the reverse: the NWT's distinctive renderings are the textual mechanism by which anti-Trinitarian theology and the divine name's centrality become built into the very Bible members read, not just preached alongside it.
figures.provenance: New World Translation of the Holy Scriptures (1950/1961, rev. 1984, 2013), New World Bible Translation Committee.

figures.gloss: The small body of anointed men directing the organization worldwide from its Warwick, New York headquarters, held to be the channel through which Christ feeds his people — Matthew 24:45's 'faithful and discreet slave,' narrowed in 2012 teaching from the whole anointed class specifically to the Governing Body itself.
figures.facet: Doctrine with a built-in update mechanism: because the 'slave' class is a living, identifiable body rather than a closed text, 'new light' can revise prior teaching at any time without contradicting the claim of continuous divine guidance — authority and doctrinal flexibility fused in one office.
figures.provenance: Matthew 24:45–47; The Watchtower, 15 July 2013 study edition (2012 identification of the Governing Body as the slave); George Chryssides, Jehovah's Witnesses: Continuity and Change (2016).

figures.gloss: Revelation 7:9's 'great crowd,' identified in Rutherford's 1935 teaching as a distinct earthly class: those who survive Armageddon or are resurrected to live forever on a cleansed, paradisal earth under the heavenly Kingdom's rule — the hope held by nearly all living Witnesses.
figures.facet: The two-tier system's larger, lived half: where the 144,000 supplies the tradition's most quoted number, the great crowd is the actual demographic reality of the movement — almost every publisher knocking on a door is preaching toward this hope, not the heavenly one.
figures.provenance: Revelation 7:9–14; J. F. Rutherford, 'The Great Multitude' (1935 discourse/publication).

figures.gloss: Judicial expulsion of unrepentant wrongdoers, decided by a committee of elders, after which members — including immediate family — are to cease normal association with the expelled individual; reframed in emphasis around 'removal from the congregation' in 2024 organizational adjustments.
figures.facet: The boundary drawn through the household itself: unlike excommunication that merely bars someone from a building, disfellowshipping is designed to be felt at the family dinner table, making organized shunning the tradition's most sociologically studied and most litigated practice.
figures.provenance: Watch Tower congregational discipline literature (Organized to Do Jehovah's Will); M. James Penton, Apocalypse Delayed (1985).

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: Shared Millerite root — Russell's faith was rekindled by Second Adventist preaching (Wendell) and his chronology came from the Advent Christian Barbour; the Watch Tower system is a grandchild of the Great Disappointment that made the advent invisible.
The 1914 chronology is Russell's signature doctrine, in print from 1876 onward — the movement's spine before and after his death.
The anointed are the personnel of the 1914 Kingdom — Christ's co-rulers in the heavenly government that will replace all earthly ones.
GOLD SEAM: Two readings of the same Levitical premise that life resides in blood — the Eucharist centers on drinking the sacred blood, the Witness taboo forbids taking blood in at all.
Two unrelated restorationist movements independently charge 1914 with decisive prophetic weight — the Watch Tower's end of the Gentile Times and the INC's registration at the war's outbreak.

Both churches' soul-sleep and annihilationist doctrine descend from the same 19th-century conditionalist matrix (George Storrs and the Life and Advent Union) that shaped Russell's own theology independently of Adventism, a shared inheritance rather than direct borrowing either way.

Two Millerite-descended reinterpretations of a disconfirmed 19th-century return-of-Christ expectation — Adventism keeps the Advent future and visible, the Witnesses relocated their 1914 expectation into an invisible, already-occurred heavenly enthronement; the same disconfirmation-and-reinterpretation move taken in opposite directions.

Two unrelated traditions whose doctrinal restriction on standard medical intervention has produced real, documented court cases over the medical treatment of minors — a structural convergence, not a shared history.
Direct organizational succession: Rutherford became Watch Tower Society president on Russell's 1916 death and rebuilt the following into the theocratic, publisher-based Jehovah's Witnesses.
Rutherford instituted the systematic house-to-house publisher system and territory assignments that make the field ministry the tradition's defining practice.
Armageddon is the enforcement action of the Kingdom enthroned in 1914 — the invisible government's decisive, still-future intervention against all human rule.
The great crowd is defined precisely as those who survive Armageddon (or are resurrected after it) into the earthly paradise — the event and its earthly beneficiaries.
The Memorial's emblems are passed to everyone present but consumed only by the anointed — the rite is the annual, visible headcount of the 144,000's remaining living members.
The NWT is used exclusively in Witness worship and door-to-door ministry, the textual instrument carried on every field-service assignment.
The Governing Body is drawn from and identified with the anointed 144,000 class, its members' authority resting on that heavenly-class status.
Elder judicial committees that carry out disfellowshipping act under doctrine and procedure set by the Governing Body — the boundary-maintenance mechanism runs on the central authority's rules.
The two halves of the tradition's two-tier salvation: a small, fixed heavenly ruling class versus the vast majority's earthly, paradisal hope — same eschatology, opposite destinations.

Independent adoptions of the same biblical term for an imminent, world-ending war — Aum Shinrikyo's Armageddon prophecy drew on general Christian/Nostradamus millenarian currents circulating in Japan, not on Watch Tower doctrine directly; parallel borrowing, not descent.

Two 19th/20th-century American movements each producing their own authoritative English-language scriptural apparatus to anchor a claim of restored or corrected truth against mainstream Christianity — though the NWT is a translation of the existing Bible and the Book of Mormon a newly revealed text, a real structural difference kept at low tier.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: two traditions independently reinterpret a biblical end-times prophecy as an invisible event already completed in a specific year, unwitnessed by the living world.

dossier.essence: Jehovah's Witnesses are the millenarian restorationist movement that grew from Charles Taze Russell's Pittsburgh Bible-student circle of the 1870s into a centralized world organization directed by a Governing Body through the Watch Tower Society. Roughly 8.8 million active 'publishers' preach in over 230 lands; the central claim is that Christ was enthroned invisibly in heaven in 1914 and that God's kingdom — a real government — will shortly destroy all human rule at Armageddon.
dossier.cosmos: The theology is non-trinitarian: Jehovah alone is Almighty God, Christ is his first creation (identified with the archangel Michael), and the holy spirit is God's active force rather than a person. There is no immortal soul — the dead are unconscious, awaiting resurrection — and no hell of torment; after Armageddon a literal 144,000 'anointed' rule with Christ in heaven while the 'great crowd' of the faithful live forever on a restored paradise earth. Satan is the present ruler of this world, which is why the Witnesses refuse it their allegiance.
dossier.practice: The defining practice is the door-to-door field ministry — members are 'publishers' who report their preaching activity — alongside weekly meetings at Kingdom Halls, baptism by immersion, and the annual Memorial of Christ's death, at which only the anointed partake of the emblems. Strict neutrality forbids voting, military service, flag salutes, and the observance of holidays and birthdays; the blood-transfusion prohibition (formalized 1945) and the practice of disfellowshipping mark the community's costliest boundaries.
dossier.canon: The Bible in the movement's own New World Translation (completed 1961), read through Watch Tower literature — above all the magazine that carried the movement from the start, The Watchtower (founded as Zion's Watch Tower, 1879).
dossier.reflects: Apocalyptic expectation made a permanent institution — a community organized to publish, sustain, and recalibrate the imminence of the end across generations of deferral.
sources: M. James Penton, 'Apocalypse Delayed: The Story of Jehovah's Witnesses' (1985)
George D. Chryssides, 'Jehovah's Witnesses: Continuity and Change' (2016)
Andrew Holden, 'Jehovah's Witnesses: Portrait of a Contemporary Religious Movement' (2002)

links.cite: M. James Penton, 'Apocalypse Delayed: The Story of Jehovah's Witnesses' (1985)
Andrew Holden, 'Jehovah's Witnesses: Portrait of a Contemporary Religious Movement' (2002)
